title: Baklava
subtitle: Layered filo pastry with walnuts and orange blossom syrup
excerpt: Thin layers of buttered filo pastry alternated with a spiced walnut filling, baked until golden and crisp, then soaked immediately with a cold fragrant syrup of honey, sugar, and orange blossom water. The contrast between the brittle, shatteringly crisp pastry and the syrup-soaked layers within is the whole point.
sourceNotes: Baklava is one of the most widely distributed pastries of the Ottoman culinary tradition, appearing across Turkey, Lebanon, Syria, Greece, Iran, and Central Asia. The Lebanese and Syrian versions typically use a honey and orange blossom water syrup with walnuts or pistachios. Claudia Roden's 'A Book of Middle Eastern Food' (1968) documents multiple regional versions. The rule of hot pastry and cold syrup (or cold pastry and hot syrup) is consistent across all traditions, the temperature contrast is what produces the crisp texture.

content.text: The technique is straightforward: butter every layer of filo generously, keep the unused sheets covered with a damp cloth at all times (they dry and crack within minutes), and score the baklava into diamonds before baking. Scoring through the top layers before baking, not cutting through, allows the syrup to penetrate later. The full cut through happens after baking, along the same lines. This prevents the filo from crumbling when cut.

The rule that produces crisp baklava: pour cold syrup over the hot baklava directly from the oven. Do not wait. The temperature shock forces the syrup into the layers and begins the crystallisation process that produces the glossy, sticky-crisp texture. A warm syrup on warm baklava produces soggy pastry.

Combine the syrup {{caster-sugar}} with 150 ml water in a saucepan. Bring to the boil, stirring until the sugar dissolves. Add the {{honey}} and {{lemon}} juice. Simmer for 5 minutes until slightly thickened. Remove from the heat, stir in the {{orange-blossom-water}}, and leave to cool completely. The syrup must be cold when it goes on the baklava.

Heat the oven to 180°C fan. Mix the {{walnuts}} with the {{cinnamon-ground}} and filling {{caster-sugar}}. Brush a rectangular baking tin (about 30 × 20 cm) with the melted {{unsalted-butter}}. Lay a sheet of {{filo-pastry}} in the tin, trimmed to fit, and brush well with butter. Repeat with 5 more sheets, buttering each one. Spread the walnut filling evenly over the top layer. Layer the remaining 6 filo sheets on top, buttering each one. Score through the top few layers in a diamond pattern with a sharp knife. Bake for 35 to 40 minutes until deep golden.

As soon as the baklava comes from the oven, pour the cold syrup evenly over the hot pastry. It will sizzle. Cut through the tin along the scored lines. Leave to rest for at least 2 hours before serving to allow the pastry to absorb the syrup and the layers to settle.

items.fix: The syrup must be cold when poured over hot baklava. Use all the syrup. It seems like too much but the filo absorbs it all. The 2-hour rest is also required for the texture to develop
items.cause: Warm syrup used or too much syrup
items.symptom: Baklava is soggy rather than crisp

items.fix: Keep unused filo sheets covered with a damp cloth at all times. Work quickly and don't leave sheets exposed for more than a minute or two
items.cause: Dried out from exposure to air

items.fix: Use a 180°C fan oven only. Place the tin on the middle shelf. If the base is browning too fast, place a second baking tray underneath as insulation
items.cause: Oven temperature too high or tin too close to the heat element
items.symptom: Bottom is burnt before the top is golden

items.fix: The full 2-hour rest is essential. The layers need time to fuse together through the syrup. Cut along the pre-scored lines with a sharp, thin-bladed knife
items.cause: Not rested long enough or cut before syrup absorbed

content.text: Baklava is one of the most argued-over foods in the Eastern Mediterranean. Turkey, Greece, Lebanon, Syria, Iran, and several other countries all claim it as theirs, and each one makes a slightly different version. Layered filo pastry spread across the region under the Ottoman Empire, and each cooking tradition shaped it to its own taste. The Lebanese and Syrian versions use orange blossom water in the syrup. The Turkish uses plain sugar syrup with lemon. The Greek adds rose water. The Iranian sometimes adds pistachios and saffron. What every version shares is the same contrast: shatter-crisp pastry on the outside, rich sweet nuts in the middle, and a fragrant syrup that holds it all together.
